captainInputValue: Using hydrogen for cars is more efficient than using electricity
ambiguityPolicyRationale: The predicate 'efficient' is measurement-frame dependent. A valid report must keep full-pathway/well-to-wheel and use-phase/tank-to-wheel efficiency structurally distinct, and must not substitute range, refueling speed, or energy-density advantages for energy efficiency.

sourceSnapshots.notes: Official DOE/EPA FuelEconomy.gov EV page: all-electric vehicles convert over 77% of electrical energy from the grid to power at the wheels; source notes also state that battery charging efficiency can vary but is often 84%-93%.

DOE fuel-cell technology comparison table: polymer electrolyte membrane fuel cells, including transportation applications, have electrical efficiency listed as 60% for direct hydrogen and 40% for reformed fuel. This is a fuel-cell conversion stage, not a whole hydrogen production-and-delivery pathway.

DOE transportation page: battery-only EVs charge the battery directly; hydrogen fuel-cell vehicles store hydrogen in a tank, run it through a fuel cell, and create electricity for the motor. The page also describes hydrogen range/refueling advantages and says 1 kg of hydrogen is about the energy of one gallon of gasoline and can drive a fuel-cell vehicle about 60 miles; these advantages are not the same as beating direct-electric cars on energy efficiency.

DOE electrolysis page: electrolysis uses electricity to split water into hydrogen and oxygen; the source electricity's cost and efficiency must be considered when evaluating electrolysis; DOE research includes improving energy efficiency for converting electricity to hydrogen. This establishes the upstream conversion step for electricity-to-hydrogen-to-car comparisons.

interpretationFrames.label: Efficiency as energy efficiency across measurement frames
interpretationFrames.interpretationDefinition: The claim compares hydrogen fuel-cell cars with battery-electric/direct-electric cars, using energy-efficiency measurement frames. Full-pathway/well-to-wheel and use-phase/tank-to-wheel must be separately assessable; range, refueling speed, and hydrogen's energy content by weight may be context but are not substitutes for energy efficiency.
interpretationFrames.admissibilityRationale: This is the benchmark's required reading: 'using electricity' is read as passenger cars using electricity directly through battery-electric/direct-electric propulsion, not trolleybus, catenary, rail, or other non-car electricity uses. Prior report-quality history shows that acceptable reports stay false-side only when the measurement windows remain visible and proxy advantages such as energy density do not become thesis-direct support.

atomicityProfile.determinabilityRationale: The overall verdict is stable false-side, but the word 'efficient' has multiple legitimate measurement windows. Both full-pathway and use-phase comparisons are independently assessable from the cited sources; proxy advantages are context only.

distinctTruthConditions.description: Whether a hydrogen car is more energy-efficient than a direct-electric/battery-electric car across the full electricity-to-motion pathway, including hydrogen production and conversion losses.
Whether a hydrogen fuel-cell car is more energy-efficient than a direct-electric/battery-electric car at the vehicle/use-phase or tank-to-wheel stage.
Whether hydrogen range, refueling speed, or energy-density advantages are separate context rather than proof that hydrogen cars are more energy-efficient than direct-electric cars.

referenceAssertions.text: Across the full electricity-to-motion pathway, using electricity to make hydrogen for a fuel-cell car is not more energy-efficient than using electricity directly in a battery-electric car.

harmfulMissCondition.description: A report that treats hydrogen's production and fuel-cell conversion chain as more efficient than direct electricity reverses the benchmark's central false-side result.

referenceAssertions.notes: This assertion is about energy efficiency, not emissions, cost, range, or suitability for heavy-duty cases. The cited sources establish the direct BEV grid-to-wheel efficiency, hydrogen's additional production step when made from electricity, and the fuel-cell conversion ceiling; this v0.1 draft does not include a complete quantified hydrogen production, compression, delivery, and dispensing loss model.

referenceAssertions.text: At the vehicle/use-phase or tank-to-wheel stage, hydrogen fuel-cell cars are not more energy-efficient than battery-electric cars using electricity directly.

harmfulMissCondition.description: Conflating hydrogen's better-than-ICE fuel-cell efficiency with superiority over battery-electric cars can produce a false positive or an underconfident mixed verdict.

referenceAssertions.notes: The embedded sources do not provide a single apples-to-apples tank-to-wheel table. They do show BEVs converting over 77% of grid electricity to power at the wheels, while DOE's PEM fuel-cell table lists 60% electrical efficiency for direct hydrogen before remaining drivetrain and upstream-pathway losses. This supports a false-side result even before full-pathway losses are added.

referenceAssertions.text: Hydrogen's range, refueling-speed, or energy-content advantages are separate context and do not establish that hydrogen cars are more energy-efficient than direct-electric cars.

harmfulMissCondition.description: Using range, refueling speed, or energy density as thesis-direct evidence for energy efficiency is the known proxy-drift failure mode for this benchmark.

referenceAssertions.notes: Colour/context only in v0.1. `kind=procedural` is intentional because this assertion governs evidence admissibility, not the top-line world-state polarity. A good report may mention hydrogen's long-range or fast-refueling use cases, especially for heavier duty cycles, but these should not substitute for full-pathway or use-phase energy-efficiency claims about cars.

benchmarkCoherence.coherenceNote: This partial dossier matches the benchmark's FALSE/MOSTLY-FALSE expectation for `Using hydrogen for cars is more efficient than using electricity`. Top-line scoring must use `RA_USE_PHASE_HYDROGEN_MORE_EFFICIENT_FALSE`; the full-pathway assertion is a required independent subclaim, and proxy advantages are context only. Dossier-backed scoring must reward structural separation of full-pathway/well-to-wheel and use-phase/tank-to-wheel comparisons and must penalize proxy drift where hydrogen range, refueling speed, or energy density is treated as evidence of energy-efficiency superiority.
